options: At least as hard as NP-Complete but may not be in NP
explanation: NP-Hard problems are at least as hard as the hardest problems in NP. Unlike NP-Complete, NP-Hard problems need not be in NP themselves â€” they may not even be decision problems.

q: Which relationship is currently believed (but not proven) to be true?
explanation: It is widely believed that P â‰  NP â€” that there exist problems whose solutions can be verified quickly but cannot be found quickly. This is the most famous open problem in computer science.

q: If P = NP were proven true, what would be the implication?
options: All NP problems would be unsolvable
All NP problems could be solved in polynomial time
explanation: If P = NP, every problem whose solution can be verified in polynomial time could also be solved in polynomial time, which would have enormous implications for cryptography and optimization.

q: The Travelling Salesman Problem (TSP) decision version is:
explanation: The decision version of TSP ('Is there a tour of cost â‰¤ k?') is NP-Complete â€” it is in NP (a tour can be verified in polynomial time) and is NP-Hard via reduction from Hamiltonian cycle.

q: What is the time complexity of the naive matrix multiplication algorithm for nÃ—n matrices?
explanation: The naive matrix multiplication algorithm performs nÂ³ scalar multiplications and additions (three nested loops), giving O(nÂ³) time complexity.

q: Little-o notation f(n) = o(g(n)) means:
options: f grows at the same rate as g
f grows strictly slower than g
f is a lower bound of g
explanation: Little-o notation means f(n)/g(n) â†’ 0 as n â†’ âˆž. It is a strict upper bound â€” f grows strictly slower than g, unlike Big O which allows equal growth rates.

q: Which of the following is TRUE about Little-omega (Ï‰) notation?
options: f(n) = Ï‰(g(n)) means f is O(g)
f(n) = Ï‰(g(n)) means f grows strictly faster than g
f(n) = Ï‰(g(n)) means f is a lower bound but not strict
explanation: Little-omega (Ï‰) is the strict version of Big Omega. f(n) = Ï‰(g(n)) means lim(nâ†’âˆž) g(n)/f(n) = 0, i.e., f grows strictly faster than g asymptotically.

q: What is the recurrence for binary search?
explanation: Binary search eliminates half the search space each step (T(n/2)) with constant work O(1) for comparison, giving T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), which solves to O(log n).

q: The aggregate method of amortized analysis:
options: Assigns different costs to each operation
Computes total cost of n operations and divides by n
explanation: The aggregate method computes the total cost T(n) for a sequence of n operations and defines the amortized cost per operation as T(n)/n. It gives a single amortized cost for all operations.

q: What is the worst-case time complexity of quicksort?
explanation: Quicksort's worst case occurs when the pivot always divides the array into one element and the rest (e.g., sorted array with first element as pivot), giving T(n) = T(n-1) + O(n) = O(nÂ²).

q: Which divide and conquer algorithm has the recurrence T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1)?
explanation: Finding the maximum element in an array using divide and conquer splits into two halves (2T(n/2)) and does O(1) work to compare the two results: T(n) = 2T(n/2) + O(1) = O(n).

q: What does it mean for a problem to be 'reducible' in NP theory?
options: It can be split into smaller subproblems
One problem can be transformed to another in polynomial time
explanation: A polynomial-time reduction from problem A to problem B means any instance of A can be transformed into an instance of B in polynomial time. If B is easy, so is A; if A is hard, so is B.

q: Space complexity considers:
explanation: Space complexity measures the total memory used by an algorithm, including the input space and auxiliary space. Sometimes only auxiliary space is counted â€” context determines which definition applies.

q: Which of the following problems is known to be in P?
explanation: Dijkstra's shortest path algorithm runs in O((V+E) log V) time using a priority queue â€” polynomial time. Graph 3-coloring, TSP optimization, and subset sum are NP-Hard/NP-Complete.

q: The Halting Problem is:
explanation: The Halting Problem (given a program and input, does it halt?) is undecidable â€” it cannot be solved by any algorithm. It is not even in NP, as it transcends the P/NP hierarchy entirely.

q: What is the time complexity of Strassen's matrix multiplication?
explanation: Strassen's algorithm has recurrence T(n) = 7T(n/2) + O(nÂ²). By Master Theorem: log_2(7) â‰ˆ 2.807. Case 1 applies: T(n) = Î˜(n^log_2(7)) â‰ˆ O(n^2.807), better than naive O(nÂ³).

q: For T(n) = T(n/2) + O(1), the Master Theorem gives:
explanation: Here a=1, b=2: log_b(a) = log_2(1) = 0. f(n) = O(1) = Î˜(n^0). Since f(n) = Î˜(n^log_b(a)), Case 2 applies: T(n) = Î˜(log n).

q: Which of the following has Î˜(n log n) as a tight bound for ALL cases?
explanation: Merge sort always divides into two equal halves and merges in O(n), giving Î˜(n log n) in all cases (best, worst, average). Quicksort is O(nÂ²) worst case.

q: What is the space complexity of quicksort in the worst case?
explanation: In the worst case (unbalanced partitions), quicksort's recursion depth is O(n), so the call stack uses O(n) space. With random pivot or median-of-3, average space is O(log n).

q: If a problem X is NP-Hard and X âˆˆ NP, then X is:
explanation: By definition, a problem that is both NP-Hard (every NP problem reduces to it) and in NP (solution verifiable in polynomial time) is NP-Complete.

q: Cook-Levin theorem states that:
options: Every NP problem is in P
explanation: The Cook-Levin theorem (1971) proves that the Boolean Satisfiability problem (SAT) is NP-Complete â€” the first problem shown to be NP-Complete, establishing the foundation for NP-completeness theory.

q: What is the time complexity of heap sort?
explanation: Heap sort builds a max-heap in O(n) time, then performs n extract-max operations each costing O(log n), giving total time O(n log n) in all cases (best, worst, average).

q: In Big O analysis, O(1) < O(log n) < O(n) < O(n log n) < O(nÂ²) < O(2â¿) represents:
explanation: This sequence represents increasing order of time complexity (growth rate). O(1) is constant (best), growing through logarithmic, linear, linearithmic, quadratic, to exponential O(2â¿) (worst).

q: Which of the following recurrences cannot be solved directly by the standard Master Theorem?
explanation: The Master Theorem requires the form T(n) = aT(n/b) + f(n) with a single recursive call of the same form. T(n) = T(n/2) + T(n/3) + n has two different-sized subproblems and cannot be directly solved by the standard Master Theorem.

q: Amortized O(1) for hash table insertion means:
options: Over many insertions the average is O(1)
explanation: Amortized O(1) for hash table insertion means that over a sequence of n insertions, the total cost is O(n), so the average (amortized) cost per insertion is O(1), even though occasional rehashing costs O(n).

q: Which problem is NP-Hard but NOT NP-Complete?
explanation: The Halting Problem is NP-Hard (every NP problem reduces to it) but it is NOT in NP because it is undecidable â€” no algorithm can verify its solution in polynomial time. Hence it is NP-Hard but not NP-Complete.

q: What is the time complexity of the KMP pattern matching algorithm for a text of length n and pattern of length m?
explanation: KMP runs in O(n+m). The failure function is built in O(m) and the search phase runs in O(n), giving total O(n+m).

q: In the KMP algorithm, the failure function for pattern 'ABABC' at index 3 (character 'B') is:
explanation: For 'ABABC': f[0]=0,f[1]=0,f[2]=1,f[3]=2,f[4]=0. At index 3, the longest proper prefix which is also suffix of 'ABAB' is 'AB', length 2.

q: Which of the following best describes the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
options: Uses a finite automaton to match patterns
Uses hashing to find pattern in text
Uses a failure function to avoid re-comparisons
Uses divide and conquer to split text
explanation: Rabin-Karp computes a rolling hash of each text window and compares it with the hash of the pattern, reducing character-by-character comparisons in the average case.

q: What is the worst-case time complexity of the Rabin-Karp algorithm?
explanation: In the worst case all hash comparisons are spurious hits requiring O(m) verification each, giving O(n*m). The average case is O(n+m).

q: In naive pattern matching, the worst-case number of character comparisons for text of length n and pattern of length m is:
explanation: Naive algorithm tries each of the (n-m+1) starting positions and in the worst case compares all m characters at each position, giving O((n-m+1)*m) approximately O(n*m).

q: The KMP failure function value f[i] represents:
options: Length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix
Number of mismatches up to position i
Position to jump in text on mismatch
explanation: f[i] stores the length of the longest proper prefix of pattern[0..i] that is also a suffix of that substring, enabling KMP to avoid redundant comparisons on mismatch.

q: In Rabin-Karp, the rolling hash technique allows the hash of the next window to be computed in:
explanation: Rolling hash computes hash of window[i+1..i+m] from hash of window[i..i+m-1] in O(1) by subtracting the leftmost character's contribution and adding the new rightmost character.

q: Which data structure is used to implement Disjoint Set Union (DSU)?
explanation: DSU is implemented as an array-based forest where each element stores its parent index. Root elements are their own parents, supporting efficient union and find.

q: What does 'union by rank' optimize in the Disjoint Set data structure?
options: Height of the resulting tree after union
Search time in a hash table
explanation: Union by rank attaches the tree with smaller rank under the root of the tree with larger rank, keeping trees shallow and reducing the cost of future find operations.

q: Path compression in DSU modifies the find operation by:
options: Splitting the path into two halves
Making every node on the find path point directly to the root
Sorting the path nodes by rank
explanation: During find(x), path compression makes every node visited on the path from x to the root point directly to the root, flattening the tree for amortized near-constant find operations.

q: The amortized time complexity per operation for DSU with both union by rank and path compression is:
explanation: With both optimizations, DSU operations have amortized O(alpha(n)) complexity where alpha is the inverse Ackermann function, effectively constant for all practical input sizes.

q: In a sparse matrix, which representation is most memory-efficient?
options: Triplet (row, column, value) list of non-zero elements
explanation: Sparse matrices have mostly zero entries. Storing only non-zero elements as (row, column, value) triplets avoids allocating memory for zeros, saving significant space.

q: In the Compressed Sparse Row (CSR) format of a sparse matrix, which three arrays are used?
explanation: CSR uses: (1) Values array for non-zero elements, (2) Column indices for each non-zero, (3) Row pointers indicating where each row's non-zeros start. This gives efficient row access.

q: A sparse matrix with n rows and k non-zero elements stored in CSR format uses space:
explanation: CSR stores k values, k column indices, and (n+1) row pointers, totaling O(k+n). This is far less than O(n*m) for a dense 2D array when k is much smaller than n*m.

q: For a 3D array A[d1][d2][d3] in row-major order, the address of A[i][j][k] (base address B, element size s) is:
explanation: Row-major order stores rows contiguously. For 3D: A[i][j][k] offset = i*d2*d3 + j*d3 + k. Address = B + (i*d2*d3 + j*d3 + k)*s.

q: In column-major order (used by Fortran), for a 2D array A[R][C], the address of A[i][j] is:
explanation: Column-major stores columns contiguously. A[i][j] is at offset j*R + i (column j, row i within it). Address = B + (j*R + i)*s.

q: Which is an advantage of dynamic memory allocation over static allocation?
options: Memory size fixed at compile time
Flexible memory usage based on runtime needs
explanation: Dynamic allocation (malloc/new) lets programs request memory at runtime based on actual needs, enabling variable-size data structures. Static allocation fixes size at compile time, potentially wasting or limiting memory.

q: Which C function allocates memory dynamically and initializes all bytes to zero?
explanation: calloc(n, size) allocates memory for n elements of given size and zeroes all bytes. malloc allocates uninitialized memory. realloc resizes an existing block. alloca allocates on the stack.

q: A memory leak occurs when:
options: Memory is allocated but never freed after use
explanation: A memory leak is when dynamically allocated memory is no longer referenced but is never freed (free/delete), causing cumulative waste and eventual memory exhaustion.

q: In static memory allocation, memory for local (automatic) variables is placed on the:
explanation: Local automatic variables use the stack. Memory is allocated when a function is called and automatically reclaimed on return. The heap is reserved for dynamic allocation via malloc/new.

q: In a circular linked list, the last node's next pointer points to:
explanation: In a circular linked list, the last node's next points back to the head, forming a circle. Unlike a singly linked list, there is no NULL terminator.

q: Which operation is more efficient in a circular linked list (with tail pointer) compared to a singly linked list (without tail)?
explanation: With a tail pointer, end insertion in a circular list is O(1): tail->next is already head, just update tail. Without a tail pointer in a singly linked list, end insertion requires O(n) traversal.

q: Traversing a circular linked list correctly requires stopping when:
options: You return to the starting (head) node
explanation: A circular linked list has no NULL. Traversal starts at head and continues until next equals head again, completing one full cycle. Any other termination condition is incorrect.

q: Which is a key disadvantage of circular linked lists?
options: Infinite loop risk if traversal termination is not handled properly
Always require double the memory of arrays
explanation: Without explicit termination conditions (checking return to head), traversal loops infinitely since there is no NULL. This is a common programming error with circular linked lists.

q: A skip list provides expected time complexity of __ for search, insert, and delete:
explanation: A skip list uses probabilistic multi-level linked lists as express lanes. Expected time for search, insert, and delete is O(log n) due to probabilistic balancing of levels.

q: How many levels does a skip list with n elements typically maintain?
explanation: A skip list maintains O(log n) expected levels. Each element is promoted to the next level with probability p (typically 0.5), resulting in expected O(log n) levels for n elements.

q: In a skip list, a new element's level is determined by:
options: A deterministic function of its key
A random coin flip at each level
explanation: Level assignment is probabilistic: after inserting at level 1, flip a coin with probability p to decide promotion to level 2, and so on. This randomness provides expected O(log n) performance.

q: Compared to a balanced BST, which statement about skip lists is TRUE?
options: Skip lists always use less memory
Skip lists are deterministic while BSTs are probabilistic
Skip lists are simpler to implement with similar average-case performance
Skip lists guarantee O(log n) worst case while BSTs do not
explanation: Skip lists are generally simpler to implement than self-balancing BSTs (AVL, Red-Black trees) and provide O(log n) expected time. However, their worst case is O(n), unlike guaranteed O(log n) of balanced BSTs.

q: A Bloom filter is used primarily to test:
options: Probable membership â€” definitely NOT in set OR possibly IN set
Frequency of elements in a data stream
explanation: A Bloom filter answers: 'definitely NOT in set' (no false negatives) or 'possibly in set' (may have false positives). It never misses a truly present element but can falsely report absent elements as present.

q: A Bloom filter with m bits, k hash functions, and n inserted elements has false positive probability approximately:
explanation: False positive probability = (1 - e^(-kn/m))^k. It decreases as m grows (more bits) and has an optimal k = (m/n)*ln(2) that minimizes false positive rate.

q: Which operation is NOT supported by a standard (non-counting) Bloom filter?
explanation: Standard Bloom filters do not support deletion because a bit may be shared by multiple elements. Clearing one element's bits would falsely remove other elements. Counting Bloom filters extend the structure to allow deletions.

q: How many hash functions does a Bloom filter use?
options: A number determined solely by set size
explanation: A Bloom filter uses k independent hash functions, each mapping an element to one of the m bit positions. The value of k is configurable and the optimal k minimizes false positive rate.

q: In the KMP algorithm, when a mismatch occurs at pattern position j (j > 0), the pattern position is reset to:
explanation: On mismatch at pattern[j], KMP sets j = failure[j-1] and retries matching at that position. This avoids re-examining text characters already confirmed to match. failure[-1] would reset j to 0.

q: Rabin-Karp is particularly efficient when:
options: Searching for a single short pattern in a short text
Searching for multiple patterns simultaneously in a long text
The pattern has all identical characters
explanation: For k patterns, hash all patterns first, then slide one window across the text and check if the window hash matches any stored hash. This gives O((n+km)) vs O(n*m*k) for k naive searches.

q: In a Disjoint Set, find(x) without path compression on a degenerate chain of n elements takes:
explanation: Without path compression, find traverses parent pointers from x to the root. In a worst-case chain (each element pointing to the next), this traversal is O(n). Path compression reduces this to amortized O(alpha(n)).

q: In DSU, which operation modifies the parent array during its execution (with path compression enabled)?
explanation: union modifies parent/rank arrays to merge two trees. find with path compression also modifies parent pointers of traversed nodes to point directly to the root, reducing future traversal depth.

q: A matrix is classified as sparse when:
options: Non-zero elements are significantly fewer than total elements
Every row has exactly one non-zero element
explanation: A sparse matrix has the vast majority of its elements as zero. When non-zero elements constitute a small fraction of all elements (e.g., less than 1/3), special storage formats save memory.

q: In triplet representation of a sparse matrix, each non-zero element is stored as:
explanation: Triplet format stores each non-zero element as (row, column, value) â€” row index first, then column index, then the value. This is the simplest sparse matrix representation.

q: For 2D array A[10][20] in row-major order (base=1000, element size=4 bytes), address of A[3][5] is:
Static memory allocation is characterized by:
options: Size and lifetime determined at runtime
Managed explicitly by the programmer using malloc/free
Size and lifetime determined at compile time, automatic management
explanation: Static allocation (global vars, static locals, compile-time arrays) has size and lifetime fixed at compile time. The runtime automatically manages stack allocation/deallocation for function-scoped variables.

q: What does realloc() do in C?
options: Allocates a new block and initializes it to zero
Resizes a previously allocated memory block
Frees memory and allocates a fresh block at same address
explanation: realloc(ptr, new_size) resizes an existing heap allocation. It may extend in place or allocate a new block, copy old data, free the old block, and return the new pointer. Returns NULL on failure.

q: A circular doubly linked list differs from a circular singly linked list in that:
options: Each node has both next and previous pointers
explanation: A circular doubly linked list has each node holding both next and prev pointers. The last node's next is head and head's prev is the last node, enabling efficient bidirectional circular traversal.

q: What is the time complexity of inserting at the beginning of a circular singly linked list given only a tail pointer?
explanation: With a tail pointer, insert at beginning in O(1): new_node->next = tail->next (current head); tail->next = new_node. No traversal needed, unlike a plain singly linked list without tail pointer.

q: In a skip list, the bottom level (level 0) contains:
options: Only minimum and maximum sentinel elements
Only elements with no level promotions
explanation: Level 0 is a complete sorted linked list of all n elements. Higher levels are sparser express lanes containing subsets selected probabilistically to speed up search.

q: A Bloom filter never produces:
explanation: A Bloom filter guarantees no false negatives: if an element was inserted, all k hash positions are set to 1, so testing always returns true for inserted elements. False positives are possible but false negatives are not.

q: Increasing the bit array size m in a Bloom filter (with n and k fixed) will:
options: Have no effect on false positive rate
explanation: More bits means bit positions are less crowded (fewer accidental 1s), so random elements are less likely to match all k positions. False positive probability (1-e^(-kn/m))^k decreases as m increases.

q: In the naive pattern matching algorithm, the pattern is shifted one position at a time, so for text length n and pattern length m the total shifts are:
explanation: The pattern of length m can start at positions 0 through n-m, giving exactly n-m+1 possible starting positions (shifts). Each shift may require up to m comparisons in the worst case.

q: Which correctly compares KMP and naive pattern matching?
options: Both have the same worst-case complexity
KMP is O(n+m) worst case; naive is O(n*m) worst case
Naive is faster in all practical cases
KMP requires O(n) extra space for the failure function
explanation: KMP guarantees O(n+m) worst case using the O(m) failure function to avoid re-examining text characters. Naive matching degrades to O(n*m). KMP uses O(m) extra space, not O(n).

q: Union by rank in DSU initializes the rank of every element to:
explanation: Initially each element is its own singleton set with rank 0 (a tree of height 0). Rank only increases when two roots of equal rank are merged, incrementing the new root's rank by 1.

q: Which application is best suited for a Bloom filter?
options: Exact key lookup in a relational database
Pre-filter to skip expensive lookups for keys definitely not in a set
Counting exact frequency of distinct elements
explanation: Bloom filters are ideal as pre-filters: if the filter says absent, skip the expensive disk/network lookup with certainty. If it says present, perform the lookup (rare false positive acceptable). This eliminates most unnecessary I/O.
